initial_claims.location: initWallet() at line 144 and initMultiowned() at line 74
initial_claims.description: Unprotected initialization functions allow anyone to take ownership of the library contract and destroy it.
initial_claims.evidence: The initWallet and initMultiowned functions are public and only protected by the only_uninitialized modifier. When the WalletLibrary is deployed, its own storage is uninitialized (m_numOwners == 0). An attacker can call initWallet directly on the library contract to become the owner, and then call kill() to execute suicide(), destroying the library and freezing all dependent proxy wallets.

claim.location: initWallet() at line 144 and initMultiowned() at line 74
claim.description: Unprotected initialization functions allow anyone to take ownership of the library contract and destroy it.
claim.evidence: The initWallet and initMultiowned functions are public and only protected by the only_uninitialized modifier. When the WalletLibrary is deployed, its own storage is uninitialized (m_numOwners == 0). An attacker can call initWallet directly on the library contract to become the owner, and then call kill() to execute suicide(), destroying the library and freezing all dependent proxy wallets.

verdict.reasoning: The `WalletLibrary` contract contains initialization functions (`initWallet` and `initMultiowned`) that are protected only by the `only_uninitialized` modifier, which checks if `m_numOwners > 0`. When the library is deployed to the network to be used by proxy contracts via `delegatecall`, its own storage remains uninitialized (`m_numOwners == 0`). An attacker can directly call `initWallet` on the deployed library contract, making themselves the owner. Once they are the owner, they can call the `kill` function, which executes `suicide` (selfdestruct). Destroying the library contract renders all proxy wallets that depend on it completely unusable, permanently freezing their funds. This is a critical vulnerability (famously known as the second Parity wallet hack).
verdict.recommendation: Remove the `kill` function entirely if the contract is intended to be used as a shared library via delegatecall, or ensure that the library contract is initialized immediately upon deployment so that no one can take ownership of the base contract.

claim_results.final_assessment: The `WalletLibrary` contract contains initialization functions (`initWallet` and `initMultiowned`) that are protected only by the `only_uninitialized` modifier, which checks if `m_numOwners > 0`. When the library is deployed to the network to be used by proxy contracts via `delegatecall`, its own storage remains uninitialized (`m_numOwners == 0`). An attacker can directly call `initWallet` on the deployed library contract, making themselves the owner. Once they are the owner, they can call the `kill` function, which executes `suicide` (selfdestruct). Destroying the library contract renders all proxy wallets that depend on it completely unusable, permanently freezing their funds. This is a critical vulnerability (famously known as the second Parity wallet hack).

The Defender has explicitly agreed with the vulnerability claim, confirming that the unprotected initialization functions allow an attacker to take ownership of the library contract and destroy it via the kill() function. No mitigations were presented to invalidate the claim.

The attacker is correct. The initialization functions are public and the library's state is uninitialized upon deployment. An attacker can call initWallet directly on the library contract to become an owner, and subsequently call kill() to destroy the library via suicide(). This is the exact root cause of the Parity Multisig hack.

The `WalletLibrary` contract contains initialization functions (`initWallet` and `initMultiowned`) that are protected only by the `only_uninitialized` modifier, which checks if `m_numOwners > 0`. When the library is deployed to the network to be used by proxy contracts via `delegatecall`, its own storage remains uninitialized (`m_numOwners == 0`). An attacker can directly call `initWallet` on the deployed library contract, making themselves the owner. Once they are the owner, they can call the `kill` function, which executes `suicide` (selfdestruct). Destroying the library contract renders all proxy wallets that depend on it completely unusable, permanently freezing their funds. This is a critical vulnerability (famously known as the second Parity wallet hack).
